content.text: Every agent in a multi-agent system needs a role. A role is not a personality or a job title — it is a precise specification that tells you exactly what the agent does, what data it consumes and produces, which tools it may call, and what it is forbidden to touch. Getting role definitions right is the most important architectural decision in a multi-agent system: a vague role leaks responsibilities across agents, causing unpredictable behaviour when the graph scales; a well-bounded role remains stable as neighbouring agents are added or replaced.

attrs.alt: Role definition components: Name, Responsibility, Tool Set, I/O Schema, Constraints — all feeding into the system prompt at runtime

content.text: The Five Components of a Role Definition
A complete role definition has five fields. Together they answer every question the graph runtime needs to route work correctly.
The name is the unique identifier used in graph edge definitions, log traces, and error messages. It should be a short, stable, lowercase noun or noun phrase:
. Avoid names that describe the model (
). The name should survive a complete internal rewrite of the agent's logic.

The responsibility is a one-sentence statement of what the agent does. The test for a good responsibility statement is: can you decide, given only this sentence and an example task, whether this agent should handle that task or not? If the answer is ambiguous, the responsibility is too broad. The word “and” in a responsibility statement is a warning sign that two agents have been collapsed into one.
"Given a list of URLs, fetch each page and return the raw HTML content."
"Fetch web pages and summarise them and store the summaries in the database."
"Given raw HTML, extract and return structured article metadata: title, author, publication date, body text."
The tool set is the minimal collection of tools the agent needs to fulfil its responsibility. Apply the principle of least privilege: a

that only reads from an external API should not receive a database write tool even if the system has one. Excessive tools increase the risk of the model calling the wrong tool, expand the attack surface for prompt injection, and inflate per-turn token cost because every tool definition is included in every API request.

The I/O schema specifies exactly what the agent receives as input and what it must produce as output. In Python the natural representation is a pair of Pydantic models. Typed I/O makes the routing logic in the graph deterministic: the orchestrator knows at compile time that
field, so the conditional edge that routes to

Constraints define what the role must not do and what happens when an edge case is encountered. They answer three questions: what is out of scope ("do not modify files, only read them"), what constitutes a failure that must be escalated ("if the API returns a 5xx, return an error state rather than retrying indefinitely"), and what resource limits apply ("process at most 20 pages per invocation"). Constraints are as important as the responsibility statement — they define the boundary that keeps the agent from drifting into its neighbours’ territory.
Translating a Role Definition to a LangGraph Node
Each component of the role definition has a direct counterpart in a LangGraph node implementation. The responsibility and constraints become the system prompt. The tool set becomes the
argument. The I/O schema becomes Pydantic models that validate the state fields the node reads and writes.

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.graph.message import add_messages


# ── I/O schema (Pydantic) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class ResearchInput(BaseModel):
    """What the researcher node expects to find in the graph state."""
    query: str = Field(..., description="Plain-language research question")
    max_sources: int = Field(5, ge=1, le=20)


class ResearchOutput(BaseModel):
    """What the researcher node writes back to the graph state."""
    sources: list[str] = Field(..., description="URLs of retrieved sources")
    excerpts: list[str] = Field(..., description="Relevant text excerpts, one per source")
    confidence: float = Field(..., ge=0.0, le=1.0,
                              description="Self-assessed confidence in coverage")


# ── Tool set (least privilege) ────────────────────────────────────────────────

@tool
def web_search(query: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Search the web and return a list of {url, snippet} results.
    Use this when you need to find sources for a research question.
    Do NOT use this to retrieve full page content."""
    # stub — replace with real search API
    return [{"url": f"https://example.com/{i}", "snippet": f"Result {i} for {query}"}
            for i in range(3)]


@tool
def fetch_page(url: str) -> str:
    """Fetch the full text content of a single web page given its URL.
    Use this after web_search to retrieve the body of a promising source.
    Do NOT use this for URLs not returned by web_search."""
    return f"<page content of {url}>"


RESEARCHER_TOOLS = [web_search, fetch_page]


# ── System prompt (responsibility + constraints) ──────────────────────────────

RESEARCHER_SYSTEM = """\
You are a researcher agent.

RESPONSIBILITY
Given a research question, find up to {max_sources} authoritative web sources
and return structured excerpts that directly answer the question.

CONSTRAINTS
- Call web_search first, then fetch_page for the most relevant results.
- Do NOT summarise or interpret the content — return raw excerpts only.
- Do NOT call any tool other than web_search and fetch_page.
- If fewer than 2 relevant sources exist, return what you found and set
  confidence below 0.5.
- Do NOT attempt to answer the question from your own knowledge.
"""


# ── Node function ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def researcher_node(state: dict) -> dict:
    inp = ResearchInput(**{k: state[k] for k in ResearchInput.model_fields if k in state})
    system = RESEARCHER_SYSTEM.format(max_sources=inp.max_sources)

    llm = ChatAnthropic(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",   # fast, cost-effective for retrieval
        temperature=0,
    ).bind_tools(RESEARCHER_TOOLS)

    # Use structured output so the response always matches ResearchOutput
    structured_llm = ChatAnthropic(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        temperature=0,
    ).with_structured_output(ResearchOutput)

    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": system},
        {"role": "user", "content": inp.query},
    ]
    result: ResearchOutput = structured_llm.invoke(messages)
    return {
        "sources": result.sources,
        "excerpts": result.excerpts,
        "research_confidence": result.confidence,
    }
Notice that the node function does not accept arbitrary
or read every key in the state dict. It reads only the keys declared in
and writes only the keys declared in
. This is the code-level enforcement of the I/O schema: the node is physically incapable of reading or writing state fields that belong to another agent.
A role and a persona are often confused because both appear in the system prompt, but they serve different purposes and evolve on different timescales.

is structural. It defines what the agent does, what data it processes, and what it is not allowed to do. It is determined at graph design time and should be stable across deployments. Changing a role means changing the graph’s data flow — the edges, state types, and possibly the tool set.

is stylistic. It defines how the agent communicates: its tone, vocabulary, level of formality, and any domain-specific voice requirements ("respond in the style of a senior engineer reviewing a pull request"). Personas can change without touching the graph structure. A customer service system might A/B test two personas for the same responder role. The role does not change; only the voice instruction at the top of the system prompt does.

Rule of thumb: if removing the instruction would change which tool the agent calls or what fields it returns, it belongs to the role. If removing it would only change how the agent phrases its output, it belongs to the persona.

# Role fragment — structural, stable across deployments
ROLE_FRAGMENT = """\
RESPONSIBILITY
Given a support ticket (subject, body, customer tier), classify it into exactly
one of: billing / technical / account / feature-request / other.
Return a ClassificationResult with fields: category, confidence, escalate_flag.

CONSTRAINTS
- Do NOT suggest solutions or respond to the customer.
- Do NOT call any external API.
- If confidence < 0.6, set escalate_flag = True.
"""

# Persona fragment — stylistic, can vary by deployment
PERSONA_FRAGMENT_A = """\
TONE
You are a precise, concise classifier. Use technical language appropriate
for an internal operations team. Do not include pleasantries.
"""

PERSONA_FRAGMENT_B = """\
TONE
You are a careful support triage specialist. Your classifications are reviewed
by human agents, so briefly note in the rationale field why you chose each category.
"""

def build_system_prompt(persona: str = "A") -> str:
    persona_frag = PERSONA_FRAGMENT_A if persona == "A" else PERSONA_FRAGMENT_B
    return persona_frag + "\n" + ROLE_FRAGMENT

The most expensive mistake in multi-agent system design is authoring roles that are coupled to the implementation details of neighbouring agents. When a neighbouring agent’s output format changes, every downstream agent that hard-codes assumptions about that format must be updated simultaneously. Roles designed for stability avoid this by treating neighbouring agents as black boxes and communicating only through typed I/O schemas.

Define the Pydantic model that describes what your node expects. Never reach into the state dict to read keys not in your schema. If an upstream agent changes its output field name, the graph integration test breaks at the edge, not silently at runtime inside your node.

Your output schema is a contract with every downstream agent. Add fields only additively (new optional fields are backward-compatible). Rename or remove fields only with a coordinated version bump. A field named
is a code smell; use a proper versioning strategy.
Constrain by function, not by neighbour identity.

Write constraints in terms of what the agent itself should or should not do ("do not write to the database"), not in terms of what another agent will do ("the summariser will handle long documents, so do not summarise"). Constraints that reference specific agents by name break whenever those agents are renamed or replaced.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal


# ── Versioned output schema pattern ──────────────────────────────────────────

class ReviewResultV1(BaseModel):
    """Output schema for the reviewer role — version 1."""
    approved: bool
    issues: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)


class ReviewResultV2(ReviewResultV1):
    """Version 2 adds severity; V1 consumers keep working (field is optional)."""
    severity: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"] | None = None


# ── Stability test: upstream changes do not break the reviewer node ───────────

class CodeWriterOutputV1(BaseModel):
    code: str
    language: str


class CodeWriterOutputV2(BaseModel):
    # Upstream renamed 'code' -> 'source_code'.  Reviewer must not break.
    source_code: str
    language: str
    line_count: int  # new field — reviewer ignores it


def reviewer_node(state: dict) -> dict:
    # Stable: reviewer only reads what it declared it needs.
    # If upstream renames 'code' -> 'source_code', the graph adapter
    # maps the field; the reviewer node itself does not change.
    code = state.get("source_code") or state.get("code", "")
    language = state.get("language", "python")

    llm = ChatAnthropic(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",   # more reasoning power for review tasks
        temperature=0,
    ).with_structured_output(ReviewResultV2)

    result: ReviewResultV2 = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": (
            "You are a code reviewer. Review the code and return a structured result.\n"
            "CONSTRAINTS: Flag issues only. Do not rewrite the code. Do not suggest style changes."
        )},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Review this {language} code:\n\n{code}"},
    ])
    return {"review": result.model_dump()}
Using Pydantic for both the input and output of a node serves two purposes. First, it validates data at the boundary — a malformed state entry raises a
before the LLM is called, producing a traceable error rather than a silent wrong result. Second, it makes the
call self-documenting: the model schema is derived directly from the Pydantic class, so the tool definition in the API request always matches the Python type.

from __future__ import annotations

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, model_validator
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from typing import Literal


# ── A richer I/O example: classification agent ───────────────────────────────

class TicketInput(BaseModel):
    ticket_id: str
    subject: str
    body: str
    customer_tier: Literal["free", "pro", "enterprise"]

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def body_not_empty(self) -> "TicketInput":
        if not self.body.strip():
            raise ValueError("ticket body must not be empty")
        return self


class ClassificationResult(BaseModel):
    category: Literal[
        "billing", "technical", "account", "feature_request", "other"
    ]
    confidence: float = Field(..., ge=0.0, le=1.0)
    escalate_flag: bool = Field(
        False,
        description="True if confidence < 0.6 or customer_tier == 'enterprise'"
    )
    rationale: str = Field(..., max_length=200)

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def auto_escalate_low_confidence(self) -> "ClassificationResult":
        if self.confidence < 0.6:
            self.escalate_flag = True
        return self


# ── Node ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM = """\
You are a support ticket classifier.

RESPONSIBILITY
Classify the incoming support ticket into exactly one category and return a
structured ClassificationResult.

CONSTRAINTS
- Do NOT draft a reply or suggest a solution.
- Do NOT call any tool.
- Set escalate_flag = True automatically for enterprise tier tickets
  regardless of confidence.
"""


def classifier_node(state: dict) -> dict:
    inp = TicketInput(**{k: state[k] for k in TicketInput.model_fields if k in state})

    llm = ChatAnthropic(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        temperature=0,
    ).with_structured_output(ClassificationResult)

    result: ClassificationResult = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": CLASSIFIER_SYSTEM},
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Ticket ID: {inp.ticket_id}\n"
                f"Customer tier: {inp.customer_tier}\n"
                f"Subject: {inp.subject}\n"
                f"Body:\n{inp.body}"
            ),
        },
    ])

    # Enterprise always escalates regardless of model confidence
    if inp.customer_tier == "enterprise":
        result.escalate_flag = True

    return {
        "classification": result.model_dump(),
        "escalate": result.escalate_flag,
    }

enforces the escalation constraint in Python, independent of the model’s response. This is the correct place for business rules that must hold unconditionally: the model handles the reasoning, but the schema enforces the invariants.

"Fetch, summarise, and store" is three roles. Each should be a separate node. Compound responsibilities produce agents that are hard to test in isolation and that fail in non-obvious ways when one sub-task fails.

Returning a plain string or a freeform dict from a node forces downstream nodes to parse text, which is fragile. Every node should return a typed Pydantic model or a dict with stable, documented keys.

Giving every agent access to every tool is a common shortcut that backfires. The model may call an unintended tool, side effects become unpredictable, and prompt injection attacks have a larger blast radius.

A system prompt with only a positive responsibility statement and no constraints leaves the agent free to call any tool, produce any output shape, and exceed any resource limit. Constraints are as important as responsibilities.

Which Claude model to use (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) is an operational decision, not a role property. The role definition should be model-agnostic. Model selection is a deployment-time configuration alongside temperature and max_tokens.
Roles that reference other agents by name.
A constraint like "if the output is too long, hand it to the summariser" couples the role to the graph topology. The correct pattern is to return an output flag (e.g.
) and let the orchestrator decide which node handles it.
Multiple clauses joined by "and"; scope unclear
Minimal; each tool justified by the responsibility
All system tools passed in "just in case"
Typed Pydantic model with field descriptions
Plain string or untyped dict with variable keys
Explicit "do not" rules; escalation conditions stated
Only positive instructions; failure modes unaddressed
Role unchanged when neighbours are added or replaced
System prompt references other agents by name
Node callable with stub I/O; output schema asserted
Can only be tested end-to-end with full graph running
External deployment config; role is model-agnostic
Model name hard-coded in the role definition
The following example defines three roles —
— each with typed I/O, a minimal tool set, and explicit constraints. The graph wires them together; the role definitions themselves contain no knowledge of each other.

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Literal, TypedDict, Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.graph.message import add_messages


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# I/O SCHEMAS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

class ResearchOutput(BaseModel):
    sources: list[str]
    key_facts: list[str] = Field(..., max_length=10)


class DraftOutput(BaseModel):
    draft: str
    word_count: int


class ReviewOutput(BaseModel):
    approved: bool
    issues: list[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
    revised_draft: str | None = None


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GRAPH STATE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

class PipelineState(TypedDict):
    topic: str
    sources: list[str]
    key_facts: list[str]
    draft: str
    word_count: int
    approved: bool
    issues: list[str]
    revised_draft: str | None
    iteration: int


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NODES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

def researcher_node(state: PipelineState) -> PipelineState:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", temperature=0
                        ).with_structured_output(ResearchOutput)
    result: ResearchOutput = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": (
            "RESPONSIBILITY: Find 3-5 authoritative sources and extract key facts.\n"
            "CONSTRAINTS: Return facts verbatim. Do not interpret or summarise."
        )},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Research topic: {state['topic']}"},
    ])
    return {**state, "sources": result.sources, "key_facts": result.key_facts}


def writer_node(state: PipelineState) -> PipelineState:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-sonnet-5", temperature=0.3
                        ).with_structured_output(DraftOutput)
    facts_text = "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in state["key_facts"])
    result: DraftOutput = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": (
            "RESPONSIBILITY: Write a 150-200 word article draft from the provided facts.\n"
            "CONSTRAINTS: Use only the provided facts. Do not add external knowledge."
        )},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Topic: {state['topic']}\n\nFacts:\n{facts_text}"},
    ])
    return {**state, "draft": result.draft, "word_count": result.word_count}


def reviewer_node(state: PipelineState) -> PipelineState:
    llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-8", temperature=0
                        ).with_structured_output(ReviewOutput)
    result: ReviewOutput = llm.invoke([
        {"role": "system", "content": (
            "RESPONSIBILITY: Review the draft for factual accuracy against the key facts.\n"
            "CONSTRAINTS: Flag only factual errors. Do not rewrite unless approved=False.\n"
            "If approved=False, return a revised_draft that fixes only the flagged issues."
        )},
        {"role": "user", "content": (
            f"Key facts:\n" + "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in state["key_facts"]) +
            f"\n\nDraft:\n{state['draft']}"
        )},
    ])
    return {**state,
            "approved": result.approved,
            "issues": result.issues,
            "revised_draft": result.revised_draft,
            "iteration": state.get("iteration", 0) + 1}


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# GRAPH ASSEMBLY
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

def route_after_review(state: PipelineState) -> str:
    if state["approved"] or state["iteration"] >= 3:
        return "end"
    return "writer"   # revised_draft becomes next draft input


graph = StateGraph(PipelineState)
graph.add_node("researcher", researcher_node)
graph.add_node("writer", writer_node)
graph.add_node("reviewer", reviewer_node)

graph.set_entry_point("researcher")
graph.add_edge("researcher", "writer")
graph.add_edge("writer", "reviewer")
graph.add_conditional_edges("reviewer", route_after_review,
                            {"writer": "writer", "end": END})

app = graph.compile()

# Run
final = app.invoke({"topic": "transformer attention mechanisms", "iteration": 0})
print(final["revised_draft"] or final["draft"])
A role definition has five components: Name, Responsibility, Tool Set, I/O Schema, and Constraints. All five must be specified before the agent is implemented.
Responsibility is a single testable sentence. The word "and" is a warning sign that two roles have been merged.
Tool sets must be minimal (least privilege). Excessive tools increase error risk and expand the blast radius of prompt injection attacks.
Pydantic I/O schemas enforce the contract between agents at the Python level, independent of the model’s behaviour.
Role (structural, stable) and persona (stylistic, swappable) serve different purposes and must not be conflated.
Roles designed for stability never reference neighbouring agents by name. Communication happens only through typed I/O schemas and conditional edges.
